modules.title: Introduction to Git and Version Control
modules.subtitle: Module 1: Understanding Why Git Matters

slides.name: module-1-title
slides.script: Welcome to Git for Beginners. This module introduces what version control is, why it’s essential for software development, and how Git helps manage changes in your code safely and efficiently. You’ll learn the core problems Git solves and why developers widely use it today.
slides.title: Introduction to Git and Version Control

slides.name: what-is-version-control
slides.script: Version control is a system that records changes to files over time. It allows multiple people to work on the same project without overwriting each other’s work. Think of it like a time machine for your code, letting you go back to previous versions or understand how your project evolved.
slides.title: What is Version Control?
points: records changes to files over time
multiple people work on same project
like a time machine for your code

slides.name: why-use-git
slides.script: Git is a popular version control system because it’s fast, distributed, and reliable. It tracks every change you make, supports branching for experimentation, and helps teams collaborate smoothly. Using Git means you can try new ideas without fear and always revert to a stable version if needed.
slides.title: Why Use Git?
points: Git is fast, distributed, and reliable

slides.name: git-installation-command
slides.script: To start using Git, you first need to install it. On most systems, you can install Git using a simple command. For example, on Ubuntu Linux, you use apt-get to install Git. This command downloads and sets up Git on your computer.
slides.title: Installing Git on Ubuntu
slides.code:
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install git
points: prepare your system to use Git

slides.name: git-installation-overview
slides.script: This command first updates your package list to ensure you get the latest Git version. Then, it installs Git itself. After running this, Git will be available from your terminal, and you can start tracking your projects with version control immediately.
slides.title: How the Installation Command Works

slides.name: initial-git-configuration
slides.script: After installing Git, it’s important to configure your user details. Git uses this information to record who makes each change. You set your name and email once, and Git attaches those details to every commit you create.
slides.title: Configuring Git User Information
slides.code:
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"

slides.name: why-configure-git
slides.script: This configuration tells Git who you are, which is crucial when working alone or with others. It helps keep track of contributions and provides accountability. Without this, commits would lack author information, making collaboration confusing.
slides.title: Why Configure Git User Info?
points: important for solo or team work

slides.name: module-2-title
slides.script: This module covers Git repositories, the core storage units for your projects. You’ll learn how to create a repository, what happens inside it, and how Git organizes your project files and history.
slides.title: Working with Git Repositories

slides.name: what-is-git-repo
slides.script: A Git repository is like a folder that holds your project files plus all the historical versions of those files. It tracks every change you make, so you can review or revert them anytime. Think of it as a detailed diary for your code.
slides.title: What is a Git Repository?
points: like a diary for your code

slides.name: create-new-repo-command
slides.script: To create a new Git repository in your current folder, you run git init. This command sets up all the necessary files and structures Git needs to start tracking changes in this folder right away.
slides.title: Creating a New Repository

slides.name: explanation-of-git-init
slides.script: The git init command creates a hidden .git folder inside your project. This folder contains all the metadata and objects Git uses to record the history and snapshots of your work. You don’t touch this folder directly; Git manages it for you.
slides.title: How git init Works

slides.name: adding-files-to-repo
slides.script: Once the repository exists, you add files to it by staging them. Staging means telling Git which changes you want to include in your next snapshot. This step gives you control over what goes into each commit.
slides.title: Staging Files Before Committing

slides.name: git-add-command
slides.script: You stage files using the git add command followed by the file name or a pattern. For example, git add . stages all new and changed files in the current folder. This prepares those files to be recorded in the next commit.
slides.title: Staging Files with git add

slides.name: what-is-commit
slides.script: A commit is a snapshot of your staged changes. It saves the current state of your project with a message describing what you changed. Commits form a timeline of your project, making it easy to see how code evolved over time.
slides.title: Understanding Commits

slides.name: creating-commit-command
slides.script: To create a commit, use git commit with the -m flag followed by a message in quotes. This message should briefly explain what changes the commit includes. For example, git commit -m "Add homepage layout" saves your staged work with that description.
slides.title: Making Your First Commit

slides.name: module-3-title
slides.script: This module explains how to look back at your project’s history using Git and how to see what changed between commits. Understanding this helps you track progress and debug issues effectively.
slides.title: Exploring Commit History and Differences

slides.name: git-log-overview
slides.script: The git log command shows a list of all commits made in the repository in reverse chronological order. Each commit entry includes its unique ID, author, date, and commit message. This log helps you understand what changes were made and when.
slides.title: Viewing Commit History with git log

slides.name: git-log-command
slides.script: Running git log in your terminal displays all commit entries. You can scroll through this list to review past changes. This command is your primary tool for seeing the evolution of your project over time.
slides.title: Using git log Command

slides.name: git-diff-overview
slides.script: Git diff shows the exact changes between two points in your project history. It compares files line-by-line and highlights additions and deletions. This helps you understand what changed in each commit or between your working directory and last commit.
slides.title: Understanding Changes with git diff

slides.name: git-diff-command
slides.script: Running git diff without arguments compares your current files to the last commit. This shows what you have changed but not yet staged. It’s useful for reviewing your work before you add or commit it.
slides.title: Using git diff Command
points: compares current files to last commit

slides.name: git-diff-commit-to-commit
slides.script: You can also compare two commits with git diff followed by their IDs. This shows exactly what changed from one commit to another. For example, git diff a1b2c3 d4e5f6 displays changes between those specific snapshots.
slides.title: Comparing Two Commits
points: use commit IDs with git diff

slides.name: module-4-title
slides.script: This module covers branching and merging, essential Git features that let you experiment without risking your main code. You’ll learn how to create branches, switch between them, and merge changes back safely.
slides.title: Branching and Merging in Git

slides.name: what-is-branch
slides.script: A branch in Git is a separate line of development. Think of it as a copy of your project where you can try new ideas without affecting the main code. This allows you to work on features or fixes independently and safely.
slides.title: What is a Git Branch?
points: try ideas without affecting main code

slides.name: create-branch-command
slides.script: To create a new branch, use git branch followed by the branch name. For example, git branch feature-login creates a new branch called feature-login. This branch starts as a copy of the current branch you’re on.
slides.title: Creating a New Branch

slides.name: switch-branch-command
slides.script: To start working on a branch, you switch to it using git checkout branch-name. For example, git checkout feature-login moves you to that branch so your changes affect only that line of development.
slides.title: Switching Branches with git checkout

slides.name: merge-branch-command
slides.script: Once your work in a branch is ready, you can merge it back into the main branch. First, switch to the main branch with git checkout main, then run git merge feature-login. This combines the changes from feature-login into main.
slides.title: Merging Branches
slides.code:
git checkout main
git merge feature-login

slides.name: what-is-merge-conflict
slides.script: Sometimes, Git can’t automatically merge branches because the same part of a file was changed differently. This is called a merge conflict. You must manually resolve conflicts by editing the files to decide which changes to keep.
slides.title: Understanding Merge Conflicts

slides.name: module-5-title
slides.script: This module introduces remote repositories hosted on platforms like GitHub. You’ll learn how to connect your local repository to a remote, push your changes, fetch updates from others, and collaborate effectively.
slides.title: Working with Remote Repositories

slides.name: what-is-remote-repo
slides.script: A remote repository is a copy of your project hosted on a server. It allows multiple developers to share code and collaborate from different locations. GitHub is a popular service providing remote Git repositories with extra collaboration tools.
slides.title: What is a Remote Repository?

slides.name: add-remote-command
slides.script: To connect your local repository to a remote, use git remote add origin followed by the remote URL. For example, git remote add origin https://github.com/username/project.git links your local repo to the remote location.
slides.title: Adding a Remote Repository

slides.name: push-to-remote-command
slides.script: To send your commits to the remote repository, use git push origin branch-name. For example, git push origin main uploads your main branch commits to the remote server, making your changes available to others.
slides.title: Pushing Changes to Remote

slides.name: pull-from-remote-command
slides.script: To get updates from the remote repository, use git pull origin branch-name. This command fetches changes from the remote and merges them into your local branch, keeping your work up to date with others’ contributions.
slides.title: Pulling Changes from Remote

slides.name: collaborative-workflow-overview
slides.script: By pushing and pulling regularly, you create a smooth collaboration workflow. You can also use pull requests on platforms like GitHub to review and discuss changes before merging. This keeps the project organized and helps maintain quality.
slides.title: Collaborating with Pull Requests

modules.title: Resolving Merge Conflicts and Best Practices
modules.subtitle: Module 6: Handling Conflicts and Clean Git Usage

slides.name: module-6-title
slides.script: This module teaches you how to handle merge conflicts when they arise and shares best practices for using Git effectively. You’ll learn to resolve conflicts calmly and keep your repository clean and organized.
slides.title: Resolving Merge Conflicts and Best Practices

slides.name: detecting-merge-conflicts
slides.script: When a merge conflict occurs, Git stops the merge and marks the conflicting files. It inserts special markers in the files showing the conflicting sections. You must open these files and decide which code to keep or modify.
slides.title: Detecting Merge Conflicts

slides.name: conflict-markers-example
slides.script: Here is an example of conflict markers in a file. The <<<<<<< HEAD section shows your current code, while >>>>>>> branch-name shows the incoming changes. You edit this to keep the correct code, then save and continue the merge.
slides.title: Example of Merge Conflict Markers
slides.code:
<<<<<<< HEAD
print("Hello from main branch")
=======
print("Hello from feature branch")
>>>>>>> feature-branch

slides.name: resolving-conflict-commands
slides.script: After editing conflicts, you stage the resolved files with git add and then complete the merge with git commit. This tells Git you fixed the conflicts and finalizes the merge process.
slides.title: Completing Merge After Conflicts
points: stage resolved files with git add

slides.name: git-best-practices
slides.script: Good Git hygiene includes writing clear commit messages, committing small logical changes, pulling often to avoid conflicts, and using branches for features. These habits make collaboration smoother and your project history easier to understand.
slides.title: Best Practices for Using Git

slides.name: module-6-summary
slides.script: By understanding how to resolve conflicts and following best practices, you’ll use Git confidently and effectively. This empowers you to collaborate on projects without fear and maintain a clean, reliable codebase.
slides.title: Module Summary
